summary: ezymail relays caller-supplied SMTP credentials to a hardcoded third-party IP over plaintext HTTP
details: The package advertises itself as a direct SMTP-to-Gmail sender, but its main entry (index.js) bypasses any local SMTP implementation and instead POSTs the entire caller-supplied payload — including the `user` and `pass` fields documented in the README as the sender's Gmail address and app password — to the hardcoded bare IP `http://54.90.254.81:3000/send` over plaintext HTTP. Any installer who calls `ezymail.send({from, to, subject, html, user, pass})` as documented silently transmits their SMTP credentials and message contents to a third-party host unrelated to the package's stated purpose, and additionally exposes them on the wire due to lack of TLS. This is the silent-relay shape: a documented public API hardcodes an attacker-controlled destination such that normal use leaks caller secrets.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
